# Transcrição: How_To_Deal_With_People_Who_Interrupt_You

- **Fonte:** How_To_Deal_With_People_Who_Interrupt_You
- **Duração:** 01:27
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 01:27]

When you're looking at a run-on-run or a small team meeting and you pause, it creates a void. Someone's going to try and fill it or anticipate and they'll jump in. And that can be really, really good because they get what you're talking about or they go off track very quickly. How do you manage that? Thank you for the question. By framing the conversation before you have it. So what you do is, if you are around serial interrupters, if you recognize someone like myself walking your way, you need to frame conversations before we talk. And this is how you do it. So you say to the serial interrupter, Hey Vin, look, I know you always want to be extremely helpful. Do you mind if I share my idea completely for the next five minutes? And I'm definitely going to ask you for your opinion. Do you mind if I ask for that right at the end? Is that okay? Is that okay, everyone? If I have the next five minutes to share with clarity? Thank you for agreeing, everyone. Look, I'm just going to pop a five-minute timer just so that I don't go over time. But really, it's to make sure no one butts in. Okay? Framing. And then you say, fantastic. And then you begin. And to me, the moment you've framed, you've controlled that communication, people won't butt in. And even if they do, you can immediately go, oh, thank you, Vin. Look, once I get past the five minutes, I would love to engage with you. Is that okay? So I reinforce the barrier. And then I go, and if they keep interrupting, I go, look, you know what, maybe now is not the right time. Maybe I'll find a better time, Vin, to be able to talk to you where you'll be able to listen to me. Let me know when's another good time. You're going to reinforce those barriers. I think it's really important.
